lines: Let's begin simply. [long pause] You're here. [long pause] That's enough for now.

Take a breath. [long pause] A real one — not the shallow, automatic kind you've been taking all day. [long pause] A slow breath in through your nose... [long pause] [long pause] two... [long pause] [long pause] three... [long pause] [long pause] four... [long pause] [long pause] five.

And out through your mouth... [long pause] [long pause] two... [long pause] [long pause] three... [long pause] [long pause] four... [long pause] [long pause] five... [long pause] [long pause] six... [long pause] [long pause] seven.
Again. [long pause] In... [long pause] [long pause] letting your chest expand, your belly soften, your ribs widen... [long pause] [long pause] four... [long pause] [long pause] five.

And out... [long pause] [long pause] releasing, like setting down something heavy... [long pause] [long pause] four... [long pause] [long pause] five... [long pause] [long pause] six... [long pause] [long pause] seven.
Once more. [long pause] In... [long pause] [long pause] slowly, filling all the way.
And out... [long pause] [long pause] all the way to the bottom. [long pause] Emptying.

Let your breathing settle into whatever rhythm it finds. [long pause] Don't control it anymore. [long pause] Just watch it for a moment, the way you'd watch waves from a shore. [long pause] The rise. [long pause] The fall. [long pause] The tiny pause between. [long pause] Your body has been breathing all day without your permission. [long pause] It doesn't need you to manage this.
Now feel your body. [long pause] Not an idea of your body — not a mental image of yourself — the actual, physical, sensory fact of you, sitting here, right now.

Start with your feet. [long pause] Feel the soles of your feet. [long pause] The pressure against the floor, or the inside of your shoes, or the surface beneath you. [long pause] The weight. [long pause] The temperature. [long pause] The contact.
Now your legs. [long pause] The heaviness of them. [long pause] The way they're positioned. [long pause] The muscles — are they tense? [long pause] Relaxed? [long pause] Somewhere in between?

Your hands. [long pause] Where are they resting? [long pause] What do they feel? [long pause] The texture of whatever they're touching. [long pause] The temperature. [long pause] The pulse in your fingertips.
Your stomach. [long pause] Rising and falling with the breath. [long pause] Soft or tight. [long pause] Full or empty.
Your chest. [long pause] The heartbeat you didn't ask for. [long pause] The lungs expanding and contracting on their own schedule. [long pause] The warmth.
Your face. [long pause] Your jaw — is it clenched? [long pause] Let it soften. [long pause] Your eyes, resting behind their lids. [long pause] Your forehead — smooth it, gently.

Feel gravity holding you here. [long pause] Feel the solidness of the surface beneath you. [long pause] You are a body, in a place, at a particular moment in time. [long pause] This is not a concept. [long pause] This is a fact. [long pause] And right now, this fact is the only thing that matters.

How much of your life do you spend somewhere other than here? [long pause] Be honest. [long pause] Replaying yesterday — the conversation you wish had gone differently, the mistake you keep revisiting. [long pause] Rehearsing tomorrow — running through scenarios, planning responses to situations that haven't happened, preparing for disasters that may never arrive.

Your body is always in the present. [long pause] Always. [long pause] It doesn't know how to be anywhere else. [long pause] It's your mind that time-travels — back and forward, back and forward — while your body sits patiently, waiting for you to come home.

There's a line from Jesus that gets quoted often but rarely understood on its own terms. [long pause] "Therefore do not worry about tomorrow, for tomorrow will worry about itself. [long pause] Each day has enough trouble of its own." Matthew six, verse thirty-four.

We hear that and think it means "don't plan ahead" or "be irresponsible about the future." But that's not what he's saying. [long pause] He's saying: stop living in a tomorrow that doesn't exist yet. [long pause] Come back to today. [long pause] There's enough here — enough trouble, yes, but also enough grace, enough beauty, enough God — to fill this moment completely. [long pause] You don't need to borrow from tomorrow.

A seventeenth-century monk named Brother Lawrence spent most of his life working in the kitchen of a Carmelite monastery in Paris. [long pause] Washing dishes. [long pause] Repairing sandals. [long pause] Peeling vegetables. [long pause] The most unglamorous, invisible, repetitive work imaginable. [long pause] And yet he described those years as the most profound spiritual practice of his life.
He called it "the practice of the presence of God."

His insight was disarmingly simple. [long pause] God is not more present in the chapel than in the kitchen. [long pause] God is not more available during prayer than during dishes. [long pause] The sacred is not somewhere else — some holy place, some spiritual mountaintop, some future version of your life where you finally have time to be devout. [long pause] The sacred is here. [long pause] In this room. [long pause] In this breath. [long pause] In this ordinary, unremarkable, Tuesday-afternoon moment.

Think of Moses and the burning bush. [long pause] It was a bush — an ordinary, scrubby plant on the side of a road in the desert. [long pause] Nothing special. [long pause] Except that God was in it. [long pause] And the miracle wasn't really the fire. [long pause] The miracle was that Moses stopped. [long pause] He turned aside. [long pause] He paid attention.

The present moment is the burning bush. [long pause] It's where God is. [long pause] Always has been. [long pause] The question isn't whether God is here. [long pause] The question is whether you'll stop long enough to notice.
So let's practice noticing. [long pause] Right now.

What do you hear? [long pause] Don't label the sounds as pleasant or annoying, wanted or unwanted. [long pause] Just... [long pause] [long pause] what is reaching your ears? [long pause] The hum of something mechanical. [long pause] A distant voice. [long pause] Wind. [long pause] Traffic. [long pause] Birdsong. [long pause] Silence itself, which has its own sound if you listen closely enough. [long pause] Whatever it is — receive it. [long pause] It belongs to this moment.

What do you feel on your skin? [long pause] The temperature of the air. [long pause] A slight draft. [long pause] The texture of your clothing against your arms, your legs. [long pause] The weight of your own hands resting. [long pause] Can you feel the air moving in and out of your nostrils — the slight coolness on the inhale, the warmth on the exhale?

What does the air taste like, smell like? [long pause] This is a sense we almost never pay attention to. [long pause] But it's here, constant, carrying quiet information about where you are, what season it is, what time of day.

This is presence. [long pause] Not an exotic state of consciousness. [long pause] Not something monks achieve after decades of practice. [long pause] Just... [long pause] [long pause] paying attention to what is actually happening, right now, in your body, in this place. [long pause] It's shockingly simple. [long pause] And shockingly rare.
Now let's go deeper. [long pause] We're going to use a practice from the centering prayer tradition — a way of being present to God that the Christian contemplatives have taught for centuries.

Choose a sacred word. [long pause] A single word that expresses your desire to be present to God in this moment. [long pause] It might be "here." It might be "peace." It might be "Jesus." It might be "rest." Or "mercy." Or "love." Or "come." Whatever rises naturally in you. [long pause] Don't overthink it. [long pause] Let it arrive.

Hold that word gently, like holding a candle in cupped hands. [long pause] It's not a mantra you're chanting. [long pause] It's not a magic formula. [long pause] It's more like an anchor — a way of returning when your mind drifts. [long pause] And your mind will drift. [long pause] That's not failure. [long pause] That's not weakness. [long pause] That's just what minds do.

The practice isn't maintaining perfect, unbroken focus — no one does that. [long pause] The practice is returning. [long pause] Every single time you notice you've wandered and gently come back to your sacred word — that return is the practice. [long pause] That is the prayer. [long pause] Not the stillness. [long pause] The returning.

Let's begin. [long pause] Settle into silence. [long pause] When thoughts come — and they will, immediately, a whole parade of them — don't fight them. [long pause] Don't be angry at your brain for thinking. [long pause] Just notice what's happening. [long pause] You might silently name it: "planning." "Remembering." "Worrying." "Judging." "Wandering." Just a soft label. [long pause] And then gently, without frustration, return to your sacred word.

Each return is an act of trust. [long pause] Each return says: I choose this moment over the imagined one. [long pause] I choose presence over distraction. [long pause] I choose the burning bush over the road.
Begin now. [long pause] Rest in the silence. [long pause] Return to your word as often as you need to.
[long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause]
If you've been pulled away, that's fine. [long pause] Come back now. [long pause] No judgment. [long pause] Just your word. [long pause] Just this breath. [long pause] Just here.
[long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause]

Stay a little longer. [long pause] This is the deep end of the practice, and it might feel uncomfortable. [long pause] It might feel boring. [long pause] It might feel like nothing is happening — like you're wasting time, sitting here doing nothing when you could be doing something productive.

But something is happening. [long pause] Beneath the noise, beneath the restlessness, beneath the mind's insistence that you should be elsewhere doing something useful — you are consenting to God's presence. [long pause] You are saying yes to being here. [long pause] And that consent... [long pause] [long pause] that is the whole prayer. [long pause] Not the perfect silence. [long pause] Not the mystical experience. [long pause] Just the willingness to stay.
[long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause]

Now, gently, let the formal practice go. [long pause] You don't need to hold the sacred word anymore. [long pause] Let it dissolve, like a word written in sand. [long pause] But stay present. [long pause] Stay in your body.
Feel your feet again. [long pause] Really feel them — the soles, the toes, the arches. [long pause] What's the sensation? [long pause] Warmth? [long pause] Pressure? [long pause] Tingling?
Feel your hands. [long pause] Open your fingers slightly. [long pause] Notice the air between them. [long pause] The weight of each finger.

Feel your chest. [long pause] The heartbeat that hasn't paused once during this entire practice. [long pause] The breath you didn't have to earn. [long pause] Your body has been here the whole time — faithful, patient, keeping you alive while your mind traveled to a hundred different places and back.

A French Jesuit named Jean-Pierre de Caussade wrote about what he called "the sacrament of the present moment." He believed that God's will is not hidden in some distant future you have to decode. [long pause] It's not a puzzle to solve, a career to build, a destination to reach. [long pause] It's here. [long pause] Right here. [long pause] In this moment, in whatever is in front of you.

The next conversation you have. [long pause] The next task you sit down to. [long pause] The next meal. [long pause] The next breath. [long pause] Each one is an encounter with the living God, if you have eyes to see it and the stillness to notice.

You don't have to go looking for God. [long pause] You never did. [long pause] God is already in the room. [long pause] In the breath you just took. [long pause] In the heartbeat you didn't request. [long pause] In the ordinary, unremarkable present that your mind keeps trying to escape.
"Each day has enough trouble of its own."

True. [long pause] But each day has enough beauty of its own too. [long pause] Enough grace. [long pause] Enough surprise. [long pause] Enough breath. [long pause] You're alive in this moment. [long pause] You're breathing, conscious, present. [long pause] That is not small. [long pause] That is the burning bush — alight with the presence of God, waiting for you to turn aside and see.

God, I confess that I spend most of my life somewhere else. [long pause] Rehearsing the future. [long pause] Regretting the past. [long pause] Running from the present because it's too ordinary, too uncomfortable, too boring to contain you. [long pause] And yet you say you're here. [long pause] You say the bush is always burning. [long pause] You say every moment is sacred ground if I'd only take off my shoes and pay attention.

Teach me to be where I am. [long pause] To see the holy in the ordinary — in the kitchen, on the commute, in the conversation I'd rather avoid, in the waiting room, in the line, in the silence I keep trying to fill with noise. [long pause] Not just in the mountaintop moments. [long pause] In the Tuesday-afternoon ones too.

Help me trust that this moment is enough. [long pause] That you are in it. [long pause] That I don't need to be somewhere else, someone else, in some other season of life to find you. [long pause] You're here. [long pause] You've always been here.

Thank you for Brother Lawrence and his dishes. [long pause] Thank you for Moses and his bush. [long pause] Thank you for every contemplative who sat still long enough to discover that the sacred isn't somewhere else — it's right where they were.
As you prepare to return to your day, take one more breath. [long pause] Slow. [long pause] Full. [long pause] All the way in. [long pause] All the way out.

Feel your body one last time. [long pause] Solid. [long pause] Present. [long pause] Here. [long pause] The weight of you on this surface. [long pause] The aliveness in your fingers and toes. [long pause] The steady, given rhythm of your heart.

As you move back into the world, carry this with you — not as a burden, not as an obligation, but as a gentle intention. [long pause] For the next hour, notice one thing you would normally miss. [long pause] One thing. [long pause] A color. [long pause] A sound. [long pause] A face. [long pause] A taste. [long pause] A shadow. [long pause] A breeze.
One burning bush, hiding in plain sight. [long pause] It's there. [long pause] It's always there.

That's the practice. [long pause] Not perfect, unbroken presence — no one lives like that this side of heaven. [long pause] Just one more moment of attention than you had yesterday. [long pause] One more turning aside to see.
